skill.name: scikit-survival
skill.description: A comprehensive toolkit for survival analysis and time-to-event modeling in Python using scikit-survival; use it when you need to model censored time-to-event outcomes, fit Cox/RSF/GB models or Survival SVMs, evaluate with C-index/Brier score, or handle competing risks.
skill.summary: Guide for using scikit-survival to model censored time-to-event data with Cox, random survival forests, and competing risks analysis.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 268 static analysis findings are false positives. Every detected pattern originates from Python code examples written inside markdown documentation files. The scanner misidentified markdown triple-backtick code blocks as Ruby shell command execution, standard Python function names as dynamic constructors, and domain terminology like 'Cumulative Incidence Function' as suspicious patterns. No executable code, network connections, filesystem access, environment variable access, or command execution exists outside of documentation examples. This skill is purely educational documentation for the scikit-survival Python library.

content.value_statement: Standard machine learning tools cannot handle censored time-to-event data, which is common in medical research and reliability analysis. This skill guides you through fitting Cox models, random survival forests, and Survival SVMs using scikit-survival, with proper evaluation using censoring-aware metrics.
actual_capabilities: Build survival targets from censored time-to-event data using Surv
Fit and interpret Cox Proportional Hazards and penalized Cox models
Train Random Survival Forests and Gradient Boosting survival models
Use Survival SVMs for margin-based survival prediction
Evaluate models with Uno C-index, time-dependent AUC, and Brier Score
Handle competing risks analysis with cumulative incidence functions
limitations: Requires scikit-survival library version 0.22 or higher to be installed in the environment
Does not support deep learning based survival models
Cannot install Python packages or access external datasets not already available

use_cases.title: Medical Survival Analysis
use_cases.description: Model patient survival after cancer treatment using censored follow-up data, fit Cox regression to identify risk factors, and evaluate with time-dependent AUC.

use_cases.title: Customer Churn Prediction
use_cases.description: Analyze customer churn as a time-to-event problem where customers who have not yet churned are right-censored. Use Random Survival Forest to identify churn drivers.

use_cases.title: High-Dimensional Genomic Modeling
use_cases.description: Fit penalized Cox models on high-dimensional genomic data with more features than samples, using elastic net regularization for feature selection and stable predictions.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Cox Model Walkthrough
prompt_templates.prompt: Walk me through fitting a Cox Proportional Hazards model on the built-in breast cancer dataset from scikit-survival. Show me how to interpret the hazard ratios and check the proportional hazards assumption.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user needs step-by-step guidance for basic survival analysis

prompt_templates.title: Model Comparison with Metrics
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me compare a Random Survival Forest with a Cox model on my dataset using Uno C-index and time-dependent AUC. Show me code for both models and explain which metric to trust for model selection.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate user comparing different survival model families

prompt_templates.title: Pipeline with Cross-Validation
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a scikit-learn pipeline for survival analysis with StandardScaler, CoxPHSurvivalAnalysis, and GridSearchCV. Use Uno C-index as the scoring metric with 5-fold cross-validation on the breast cancer dataset.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user building reproducible survival analysis workflows

prompt_templates.title: Competing Risks Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through competing risks analysis using cumulative incidence functions. Use the leukemia dataset where patients can experience relapse or death. Show how to estimate and plot the CIF for each event type.
prompt_templates.scenario: Expert user analyzing studies with multiple mutually exclusive event types

output_examples.input: Fit a Cox Proportional Hazards model on the breast cancer dataset and tell me which features are most important.
output_examples.output: The Cox model identified three key predictors of shorter survival: tumor size (hazard ratio 1.45, p < 0.01), number of positive lymph nodes (hazard ratio 1.32, p < 0.01), and progesterone receptor status (hazard ratio 0.72, p < 0.05). Positive coefficients indicate higher risk. The model achieved a Uno C-index of 0.78, meaning it correctly ranks patient risk pairs 78 percent of the time.

output_examples.input: Compare a Random Survival Forest with a Cox model on this dataset.
output_examples.output: The Random Survival Forest achieved a time-dependent AUC of 0.81 at 365 days versus 0.76 for Cox regression. The Cox model provides interpretable hazard ratios for each feature, while the forest captures non-linear interactions but does not give coefficient-level explanations. For your goal of identifying risk factors, I recommend the Cox model. For pure prediction accuracy, the forest performs better.

output_examples.input: Explain the competing risks analysis output for the leukemia treatment study.
output_examples.output: The competing risks analysis shows two cumulative incidence curves: relapse (event type 1) and death in remission (event type 2). At 52 weeks, the cumulative incidence of relapse is 35 percent, while the cumulative incidence of death in remission is 12 percent. These curves properly account for the fact that death prevents the observation of relapse, unlike a standard Kaplan-Meier estimate which would overestimate relapse probability.

best_practices: Always scale features before fitting Survival SVMs or penalized Cox models to ensure proper regularization and gradient convergence.
Use Uno C-index instead of Harrell C-index when the dataset has heavy censoring, as Uno method uses inverse probability of censoring weights for a more robust estimate.
Verify that the number of events is sufficient relative to the number of features to avoid overfitting, especially with Random Survival Forests.
anti_patterns: Using Kaplan-Meier to estimate event probability when competing risks are present, which overestimates risk by treating competing events as censored observations.
Evaluating survival models with standard accuracy or ROC AUC metrics that do not account for censored observations.
Ignoring the proportional hazards assumption check for Cox models, which can lead to biased coefficient estimates and incorrect conclusions.

faq.question: What is censored data in survival analysis?
faq.answer: Censored data occurs when the event of interest has not happened by the end of the study or when a subject leaves the study before the event occurs. Standard machine learning cannot handle this type of incomplete information.

faq.question: What is the difference between Harrell C-index and Uno C-index?
faq.answer: Harrell C-index measures how well the model ranks risk pairs but can be biased with heavy censoring. Uno C-index uses inverse probability of censoring weights for a more robust estimate when censoring is present.

faq.question: Can I use scikit-learn tools with scikit-survival models?
faq.answer: Yes. scikit-survival implements a scikit-learn compatible API. You can use Pipelines, cross-validation, GridSearchCV, and other scikit-learn utilities with all survival models.

faq.question: What is a competing risk?
faq.answer: A competing risk is an event that prevents the event of interest from occurring or changes its probability. For example, in a cancer study, death from heart disease competes with death from cancer.

faq.question: Do I need to install anything to use this skill?
faq.answer: You need scikit-survival version 0.22 or higher installed in your Python environment, along with scikit-learn, numpy, and pandas.

faq.question: What does the Integrated Brier Score measure?
faq.answer: The Integrated Brier Score measures prediction error across all time points. It combines both discrimination and calibration into a single score, with lower values indicating better predictions.
